era_context: Continuation of the Classical Greek period and early 4th century BCE. The Corinthian War remains active; Sparta continues to assert hegemony over Greece despite opposition from Thebes, Athens, and Corinth. Rome is recovering from the Gallic sack and consolidating its position in Latium. The Persian Achaemenid Empire under Artaxerxes II is stabilizing after internal conflicts and the Anabasis of Cyrus the Younger.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Detailed records from most of the Mediterranean beyond Greece and Rome

events.title: Corinthian War: continued military campaigns
events.description: The Corinthian War continued with ongoing campaigns between Spartan forces and the anti-Spartan coalition (Thebes, Corinth, Athens, Argos). Naval operations in the Aegean and land campaigns in central Greece characterized this year. Persian financial support for anti-Spartan factions remained a key factor in sustaining the conflict. The war was approaching its resolution through diplomatic negotiation.
events.certainty_note: Xenophon's near-contemporary account confirms the continuation of the Corinthian War through this period.
cross_references: Part of the Corinthian War (395–387 BCE)

events.title: Athenian naval operations in the Aegean
events.description: Athens, supported by Persian gold, conducted naval operations in the Aegean to challenge Spartan naval dominance and support allied cities. These operations were part of the broader Corinthian War strategy to weaken Spartan hegemony. Athenian naval power, though diminished from its 5th-century peak, remained significant in regional politics.
key_figures: Artaxerxes II (Persian Great King, financial supporter)
events.certainty_note: Athenian naval activity during the Corinthian War is attested in sources, though specific operations in 389 BCE are not always clearly distinguished from adjacent years.
cross_references: Part of the Corinthian War (395–387 BCE)
Related to Persian support for anti-Spartan factions

events.title: Roman consolidation in Latium and conflict with neighboring tribes
events.description: Following the Gallic sack of 390 BCE, Rome was consolidating its position within the Latin League and managing relations with neighboring Italic tribes. This period saw Roman military reorganization and the beginning of Rome's gradual expansion beyond Latium. Specific military campaigns in 389 BCE are poorly documented, but the general trajectory of Roman consolidation and expansion is clear from later sources.
key_figures: Camillus (Roman military leader and statesman, though his exact activities in 389 BCE are unclear)

sources.name: Livy, History of Rome (Ab urbe condita)

events.certainty_note: The general context of Roman consolidation is clear, but specific events in 389 BCE are not well-documented. Livy's account is written centuries after the fact and may conflate or misdate events.
cross_references: Follows from the Gallic sack of 390 BCE
Part of Rome's gradual expansion in the 4th century BCE

disconfirming_evidence: No major disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. However, the sparse documentation for Roman activities in 389 BCE reflects a broader historiographic problem: Roman history of the 4th century BCE is poorly attested, and much of what Livy reports may be legendary or misplaced chronologically. The Corinthian War is well-attested, but specific campaigns in individual years are sometimes difficult to distinguish in the sources.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 389 BCE is dominated by Greek sources, particularly Xenophon's Hellenica, which provides a near-contemporary but Athenian-centric perspective. Roman history of this period is almost entirely dependent on Livy, written centuries later, and is therefore inherently unreliable for specific events and dates. Persian sources are minimal. The record is heavily weighted toward Greece and Rome; the broader Mediterranean, Near East, and all other regions are underrepresented or absent.

graph_edges.note: The Corinthian War continued toward its resolution in the King's Peace of 387 BCE
Roman consolidation followed the Gallic sack of 390 BCE
